# Questions in Livestream 77

Live Stream #77: Well, It Happened. Open Room Special.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:07</a> | Will he ever write a book on Old Testament reliability, and would it be written for lay readers? | He said any such book would be made accessible for ordinary readers, though he has not decided yet whether to write it because Kitchen already covered the ground well. | He says accessibility would be the easy part because he generally tries to bring high-level scholarly topics down to ordinary readers. The uncertainty is whether to write that particular book at all: Kitchen has already written a strong book on Old Testament reliability, and Falk has other book projects he would prioritize first, including Exodus, the plagues, and chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=883" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:43</a> | When will he be able to complete the book on the Ten Plagues? | He said the funding has not been there, so the project depends on funding and other opportunities. | He thanks the questioner for enjoying the Ten Commandments series, then explains that the Ten Plagues book has not moved forward because the needed funding has not been available. He frames it practically: the project has to follow funding and employment opportunities, so he cannot give a completion date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=1292" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:32</a> | What was Sean McDowell's position before the debate, and did the debate change his mind? | He said McDowell had been an early dater before the debate, but McDowell did not say afterward whether his position had changed. | Falk says the main unknown was McDowell's reaction after moderating the debate. According to Falk, McDowell had held an early-date position beforehand, but he did not disclose afterward whether the debate had shifted him, so Falk leaves the result unstated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=1323" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:03</a> | What debate is he talking about, and where can viewers watch it? | He explained that it was his discussion with Titus Kennedy on Sean McDowell's channel and said it had been recorded but not yet aired. | He identifies the debate as Falk versus Titus Kennedy on the date of the Exodus, hosted on Sean McDowell's channel. Falk says he had understood it would be live that day, but it was only recorded and was expected to air the following week, so viewers had not missed it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=1409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:29</a> | Is Titus Kennedy connected with Associates for Biblical Research? | He said Kennedy is not an associate with them, but is closely affiliated through excavation work, public speaking, articles, and recommendations. | Falk distinguishes formal membership from practical affiliation. He says Kennedy is not an associate of ABR, but is closely connected because he works at their excavation sites, speaks for them, writes articles for them, and recommends them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=1452" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:12</a> | Why was Stripling, rather than Titus Kennedy, in the *Five Views on the Exodus* book? | He said publishers tend to choose older, more established representatives for those multiview volumes. | Falk suggests the choice was likely about status and representation rather than a claim that Stripling is much better informed. In his view, those books tend to select older or more established figures for each position, and Kennedy was newer on the scene. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=1669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:49</a> | Did the *Five Views on the Exodus* book include the documentary hypothesis? | He said not really; Hendel broaches it and Finkelstein likes the documentary hypothesis, but it was not really discussed. | Falk says the documentary hypothesis was not a central topic in the book. He notes that Hendel only broaches it and Finkelstein favors it, but the volume was divided between arguing for Exodus dates and arguing about whether there was an Exodus at all, so the documentary hypothesis did not receive real treatment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=2155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:55</a> | Did Titus Kennedy bring up Jephthah from Judges 11 in the debate? | He said yes, and criticized Kennedy for treating the Amorites as Jephthah's primary adversaries instead of the Ammonites. | Falk says Kennedy did raise Jephthah, but Falk was surprised by how Kennedy used the passage. He argues that Kennedy should know Joshua and Judges well because of his conquest-period work, yet Kennedy treated the Amorites as Jephthah's main adversaries when Falk says the passage is about the Ammonites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=2487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:27</a> | Did Kennedy bring up the First Chronicles 6 genealogy argument? | He said Kennedy did bring it up but conceded the point, because the genealogy is spliced around Samuel's Ephraimite line. | Falk confirms that Kennedy raised the First Chronicles 6 genealogy, but says Kennedy did not fight the point once challenged. The discussion treats the genealogy as a spliced or honorific genealogy: it begins in a Levitical context but includes Samuel's Ephraimite line, so Falk does not accept it as a straightforward chronological count. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=3131" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:11</a> | Did Kennedy bring up evidence that Falk did not have a chance to counter? | He said most of it was standard, though Kennedy brought up some things he did not expect, such as Petrovich's interpretation of the smashing-idols stela. | Falk says most of Kennedy's material was standard and therefore expected. The example that surprised him was Kennedy's appeal to Petrovich's reading of a stela about smashing idols, an argument Falk says he did not expect Kennedy to use. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=3170" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:50</a> | Had Petrovich recently published on the smashing-idols argument? | He said it appears in Petrovich's book and that Petrovich had also written a paper on it. | Falk says the argument is in Petrovich's book and that Petrovich had also written a paper on it. The surrounding discussion then turns critical of the book's presentation and publication route, but the direct answer is that the material existed in both book and paper form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=3466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:46</a> | Is Titus Kennedy aware that the late date can be framed as the conservative view? | He said Kennedy is aware now because it came up in the debate. | Falk answers that Kennedy is aware now because the point was raised during the debate. The broader issue is that Falk frames his own late-date position as compatible with an evangelical conservative view of Scripture, not as a liberal compromise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=3487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:07</a> | What is the broader history of the early-date versus late-date Exodus debate? | He said the debate has old roots, but chronology was not always pressed as hard in earlier Christian discussion as it is now. | Falk says early Jewish, Christian, and Greek chronological work did not always have secure historical anchors, so ancient chronological arguments can be badly off. He gives the Seder Olam Rabbah as an example, saying it assigns about 50 years to the Persian period when the period was closer to 250 years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=3634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:34</a> | Did Kennedy concede any of Falk's arguments? | He said sometimes Kennedy seemed to concede, but then would minimize a point by saying only that some people believed it. | Falk says Kennedy's concessions were strange: at points he seemed to grant the force of an argument, but then softened it by saying only that some people believed the point or that he would not make it primary. Falk treats that as functionally conceding while trying to limit the damage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=3664" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:04</a> | Is the early-date Exodus scheme connected to Ussher's creation chronology or a six-thousand-year framework? | He said not really; Ussher worked from a different chronological starting point, while many modern early daters work backward from Solomon's temple. | Falk separates Ussher from modern early-date reasoning. He says Ussher was adapting Scaliger's chronology into an Anno Domini framework from a 4004 BC creation date, whereas modern early daters usually work backward from the temple date and the biblical numbers attached to Solomon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=3983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:23</a> | Did Sean McDowell show bias in the debate questions? | He thought there was some mild bias and even one attempted lifeline, though he did not frame it as overt bad faith. | Falk says he could feel a slight bias in some of McDowell's questioning. His clearest example is a moment when McDowell tried to give Kennedy a more generous framing, but Kennedy rejected that help by affirming the stricter version of his own view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=4560" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:00</a> | What would McDowell say if told that the late date was historically the conservative view? | He said McDowell likely got that message during the debate, though the broader perception still treats the late date as the liberal or compromise view. | Falk says McDowell probably heard that argument in the debate, but he is unsure whether it would change McDowell's perception. Falk emphasizes that the public perception remains strong: many still treat the late date as liberal or compromising even if Falk argues otherwise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=4838" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:38</a> | What about David Rohl supporters? | He said he still gets some negative mail from Rohl supporters, but not as much as before and less than from Ron Wyatt supporters. | Falk says Rohl supporters still send him occasional hostile mail, but less than they once did. He contrasts that with Ron Wyatt supporters, whom he says are currently much more active in sending him negative messages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=5443" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:43</a> | Did the debate include any revisionist Amarna letters interpretations? | He said no; the Amarna letters were barely touched on, and only in terms of chronology rather than revisionist interpretations of Canaan. | Falk says no revisionist Amarna-letter interpretation appeared in the debate. He says the Amarna letters were mentioned only briefly, and when he used them it was for chronology rather than for a detailed reconstruction of what was happening in Canaan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=6174" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:54</a> | Would Christians, regardless of Exodus-date position, find any edifying or evangelistic benefit in the debate? | He said that is hard to say because the debate structure was narrow, brief, and did not allow much introductory or discipleship-oriented framing. | Falk says the debate's edifying value is hard to judge because the structure was mostly Kennedy's and tightly focused. He wanted more context and introduction, but the debate largely introduced one early-date archaeologist and one late-date Egyptologist, then moved straight into a narrow Bible-focused exchange. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=6454" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:34</a> | Did Kennedy use the Berlin pedestal or Israel inscription? | He said Kennedy did not use the Berlin pedestal, though he wanted to bring in the Shasu of Yahweh. | Falk says Kennedy did not use the Berlin pedestal. He adds that Kennedy wanted to bring in the Shasu of Yahweh, but apparently ran out of time, so Falk discusses the argument from Kennedy's known use of it elsewhere rather than from the debate itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=6474" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:54</a> | How does Kennedy defend using the Shasu of Yahweh? | He said Kennedy treats the Soleb inscription as evidence for Israelites in the wilderness under Amenhotep III, but Falk argued that the identification and biblical geography both create problems. | Falk says Kennedy's argument is that the Soleb inscription mentions the land of the Shasu of Yahweh in Amenhotep III's reign and therefore points to Israelites wandering in the wilderness. Falk rejects that as too strong: the name may or may not be Yahweh, and even if it is, he argues it does not automatically identify the Shasu as Israelites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=6785" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:05</a> | Does the Shasu inscription connect to the Kenite hypothesis of Yahweh's origins? | He said the Kenite hypothesis, in which Yahweh worship originated in Midian, is reasonable given Moses' Midian context and the broader late-Bronze-age extent of Midian. | Falk says the Kenite hypothesis is not unreasonable because late-Bronze-age Midian was much larger than later Midian, extending from Arabia through Edom into eastern Sinai and including groups such as Shasu. In that framework, Moses' encounter with Yahweh in Midian and return to Egypt with Yahweh worship makes contextual sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=7092" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:12</a> | Does the Kenite hypothesis help or undermine the Exodus account? | He said it does not decide the truth of Exodus either way, though it helps make sense of some of the data better than putting Israelites in Edom. | Falk says the hypothesis is neither proof nor disproof of Exodus. He thinks it can help account for some Yahweh-origin data and is preferable to forcing Israelites through Edom, but the truth of the Exodus account does not depend on whether the Kenite hypothesis is accepted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=7199" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:59</a> | How does he evaluate reading Exodus 6:3 as a question? | He said Hebrew can use unmarked questions, but he wants good warrant before turning a declarative sentence into a question because that can invert the meaning. | Falk acknowledges that Hebrew can form a question without an interrogative marker, so the proposed reading is possible in principle. His concern is methodological: changing a declarative sentence into a question can reverse its meaning, so he wants strong contextual warrant before accepting that move. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=7481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:41</a> | Does Dwayne Garrett's chiastic reading strengthen the question interpretation of Exodus 6:3? | He said the proposed chiastic structure looked strange and that the breakdown seemed odd. | Falk's response to the chiastic proposal is skeptical rather than fully developed on the spot. He agrees unmarked questions exist, but when shown Garrett's chiastic defense he says the structure looks strange, especially the proposed parallels between God's self-identification and the disputed question reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=7656" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:36</a> | What about Garrett's translation of Exodus 3:14 as "This is who I am, I am"? | He said there is a grammatical reason to distinguish the first-person and third-person forms behind "I am" and Yahweh. | Falk says the translation sounds odd, then turns to the grammar behind the divine-name forms. He explains that there is a real grammatical reason to distinguish forms such as the first-person "I am" and the Yahweh form, involving Semitic verbal or copular tense distinctions as dialects meet in the Sinai context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=7891" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:31</a> | Is Moses asking God's name in Exodus an Egyptian-religion polemic? | He said Egyptian religion distinguished public and private divine names, so the question is contextually interesting, but Yahweh's answer still must be handled carefully. | Falk says Egyptian deities could have public and private names, and knowing a private name was connected with power, magic, and control. That makes Moses' question culturally interesting, but Falk does not simply reduce Yahweh's answer to an Egyptian-name polemic; he treats the context as illuminating while still requiring careful exegesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=8430" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:30</a> | Would he debate Ron Hendel, and would he do it on MythVision? | He said he would not do it on MythVision because of a prior bait-and-switch, but he would not mind taking a crack at Hendel elsewhere. | Falk says he is unwilling to do the debate on MythVision because of what he calls the Josh Bowen bait-and-switch experience. He is not afraid to debate Hendel, however, and says he would be willing to engage him in another venue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=8915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:35</a> | Is Yahweh a name or a divine designator? | He said it is both a name and a divine designator, though more specificity would be needed for a longer answer. | Falk first asks for clarification because "divine nominator" is not a category he recognizes, then answers once the wording is clarified to "divine designator." His answer is that Yahweh can be both a name and a divine designator, but a more precise question would be needed for a fuller treatment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=9465" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:45</a> | What should be done with the mention of Ramses in Genesis? | He treated it as retrojection: Genesis uses a later, recognizable term to identify Goshen as the ancestral homeland of the Ramesside clan. | Falk says the reference to Ramses in Genesis is a retrojective place-name, not a problem to solve by moving the patriarchal story into the Ramesside period. He explains that Genesis identifies Goshen with the later territory of Ramses, around Avaris, because that was the ancestral homeland of the Ramesside clan and helps readers locate the region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=10021" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:01</a> | Does Genesis 1:1 reveal how the universe works, or are such readings user projections? | He said Genesis 1-2 is mainly about sacred space and temple imagery, not a technical account of how the universe was created. | Falk agrees that Genesis 1:1 is not trying to reveal technical mechanics of the universe. He says Genesis 1-2 is shaped around sacred space and temple imagery, giving a religious and liturgical message rather than a scientific account of cosmic processes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=10324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:04</a> | Could the Ramessides have been Semitic, given their Delta origins and names? | He said probably not in the paternal line, though intermarriage with Semitic women was possible and the Ramessides likely knew Semitic dialects from growing up in Avaris. | Falk says the Ramesside family probably did not have Semitic roots through the paternal line because their land grants fit Egyptian military rewards after the expulsion of the Hyksos. He allows possible Semitic ancestry through intermarriage and says their Avaris background likely exposed them to Amorite or other Semitic dialects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=11100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:00</a> | Is Daniel's use of the name Arioch comparable evidence for an older setting or source? | He said names are tricky, but Arioch is specific enough that it raises a legitimate question for a purely Maccabean-period explanation of Daniel. | Falk accepts the comparison cautiously because names can be complicated evidence. Still, he says Arioch is tied to a specific time and place and would likely require knowledge of cuneiform traditions, so its presence in Daniel creates a real question for an explanation that makes the name arise only in the Maccabean period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/typXFqnn9dw?t=11450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:50</a> | Is Ipuwer the text that *Patterns of Evidence* connects to the Exodus plagues? | He said yes, but argued that Ipuwer belongs in the Middle Kingdom, not the 18th Dynasty, based on language and historical markers such as pyramid building. | Falk says *Patterns of Evidence* connects Ipuwer to the Exodus because of plague-like details such as water turning to blood and darkness. He rejects the dating required for that connection: Ipuwer's base language is Middle Egyptian, copyist updates do not determine the original date, and internal markers such as pyramid building place it in the Middle Kingdom rather than Dynasty 18. |
